danger_signals.visual_form: Digitized sprite opponents with martial arts poses and menacing stances
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat requiring engagement in combat

danger_signals.visual_form: Large, prominent mummy character sprite with supernatural/undead visual treatment
danger_signals.meaning: Major narrative danger; primary antagonist and source of world corruption

danger_signals.visual_form: Character sprite crossing between world contexts (Tokyo charity game to Second World return)
danger_signals.meaning: Threat breach: danger has escaped containment and entered the player's origin world

danger_signals.visual_form: Genesis version includes 5 additional characters (Auroch, Colonel, Diesel, Leotsu, Nezu) vs SNES; visual sprite differentiation
danger_signals.meaning: Different threat roster depending on version; expanded danger scope in Genesis platform

safety_signals.meaning: Sanctuary space before dimensional breach; place of mastery and preparation

safety_signals.visual_form: Stage transition back to Tokyo charity basketball game context
safety_signals.meaning: Restoration of normal world-state; successful navigation of interdimensional threat

safety_signals.visual_form: Digitized Shaquille O'Neal sprite with martial arts competency demonstrated through gameplay
safety_signals.meaning: Player agency and combat efficacy as counter to danger

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple unique character designs (Auroch in Genesis, Colonel, Diesel excluded from SNES); Beast as franchise representative in origin world
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Second World dimension setting; The Lab, The Wasteland, Yasko Mines stages (Genesis) represent corrupted/dangerous spaces

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Tokyo dojo; basketball game as cultural institution
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Beast (Sett-Ra minion) disguised as opposing basketball team member; represents faction contamination
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Tokyo charity game location; represents uncontaminated normal world state

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Dojo setting; Leotsu as martial arts master NPC
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Shaq mistaken identity as 'warrior chosen by the Stars' — visual/narrative confusion central to plot
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Dojo in Tokyo; North Gate/South Gate stage (available Genesis default, SNES cheat-code only)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Tokyo dojo and charity basketball game contexts; digitized realistic sports/martial arts setting
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Shaq travels to charity event; Leotsu conducts martial training; no supernatural hostility

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Second World dimension accessible; shift to fantastical stage environments (Lab, Wasteland, Yasko Mines in Genesis); mummy aesthetics dominate
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Shaq trapped in alternate dimension; forced into combat against Sett-Ra and minions; rescue mission for Nezu (child hostage)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Return to origin world basketball game; Beast sprite now appearing as opposing team member
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Beast initiates rematch; contamination persists — threat not fully contained despite primary boss defeat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Genesis: 5 additional playable characters + 3 bonus stages = expanded threat ecosystem; SNES: reduced roster = contained threat narrative
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Difficulty and character variety modulates per platform; longer story mode (Genesis) implies more elaborate danger progression

environmental_storytelling.example: Charity basketball game (mundane, safe) vs. Second World with mummy boss and corrupted stages (supernatural, dangerous); contrast communicates narrative stakes

environmental_storytelling.technique: Stage environment naming as worldstate indicator
environmental_storytelling.example: 'The Wasteland' and 'The Lab' (Genesis) signal dangerous, unnatural spaces; 'Yasko Mines' suggests resource-exploitation corruption; North/South Gate implies boundary crossing

environmental_storytelling.technique: Character sprite design as faction/threat identity
environmental_storytelling.example: Sett-Ra mummy design; Beast's dual-context appearance (minion in Second World, basketball player in origin); Auroch and Colonel visual distinctness (Genesis exclusives) expand threat taxonomy

environmental_storytelling.technique: Hidden blood code as world-state modifier
environmental_storytelling.example: Unlockable 'blood code' (Genesis/SNES) reveals finishing moves; visual bloodletting signals breach of restraint — world has degraded to lethal combat necessity
Shaq mistaken for 'warrior chosen by the Stars' — visual/narrative confusion triggers dimensional event; identity ambiguity manifests danger

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Traveling to charity event; conversing with Leotsu; martial arts interest signaled but non-combative
Forced into continuous combat; rescue mission-driven; must defeat Sett-Ra and minions to achieve dimensional return
Encounters Beast rematch at basketball game; world-state threat persists despite primary victory
Commands minions; holds Nezu hostage; guards Second World dimension; defeated by protagonist
Penetrates origin world; infiltrates basketball game as opposing team member; initiates rematch desire — suggests sentience and persistent threat agency
Identifies Shaq; unknowingly triggers misidentification as 'chosen warrior'; facilitates interdimensional breach through mistaken authentication
Held captive by Sett-Ra; represents innocent life requiring rescue; liberation motivates protagonist action
Serve Sett-Ra; provide progressive combat challenges; visual distinctness (Genesis expanded roster) modulates difficulty progression and world threat complexity

notes: Shaq-Fu communicates world-state through dimensional context-switching (normal Earth vs. supernatural Second World), protagonist capability signaling via digitized sprite realism, and threat persistence despite narrative victory (Beast rematch ending). The game lacks explicit faction symbols or color-coding typical of fighting games; instead, threat identity derives from character design (mummy aesthetics for Sett-Ra), stage environment naming/visuals (Wasteland, Lab, Mines), and cross-dimensional intrusion narrative. Platform variations (Genesis vs. SNES character/stage differences) create version-specific threat scopes, suggesting danger magnitude is medium-dependent. The hidden blood code represents world-state degradation to lethal violence necessity. Protagonist misidentification serves as core plot engine — visual/identity confusion manifests danger mechanically. Minimal explicit political faction structure; threat is fundamentally cosmological (dimensional breach) rather than ideological.
